result:
The afternoon smells like thunder, though the sky is still clear—an ache of ozone and hot asphalt curling up from the road the way memories rise when you’re not guarding against them. I’m walking nowhere in particular, just tracing the seam between town and field, where sidewalks surrender to bindweed and the wild grasses write their own cartography. There’s a hush that feels global, as if every radio tower paused mid-breath, letting the planet’s pulse go suddenly audible: cicadas, a distant tractor, my own sneakers scuffing grit louder than I’d like.  

I keep thinking about the word enough. How it arrives uninvited in childhood—You’re not trying hard enough, we don’t have enough, when will it be enough?—and then follows you like a quiet debt collector, slipping its bills under every door you open. Yesterday I watched a toddler fling fistfuls of gravel skyward, shouting “More!” each time, and I realized greed is just hunger wearing its first clumsy costume. We learn to call it ambition, then maturity, then legacy, but the syllables don’t matter; the throat stays the same raw size.

At the edge of the field stands an abandoned billboard, its aluminum ribs showing through torn vinyl like ribs of a whale picked clean by translators. Someone has spray-painted in neon coral: YOU ARE ALREADY HERE. I can’t decide whether it’s prophecy or mockery. I photograph it anyway, because I was raised to collect evidence, as if proof could be traded for entry into some brighter wing of the self. My phone screen catches the sun and throws it back, a white-hot slap that makes me blink purple galaxies onto the inside of my eyelids. For a moment, the afterimage is the only galaxy I believe in.

When I lower the phone, a grasshopper the size of my thumb is balancing on the margin of my shadow. Its wings are the color of oxidized copper, and I remember that copper conducts both heat and electricity, that the Statue of Liberty is green for the same reason my palms sometimes feel like they’re holding an invisible live wire. I wonder what it’s like to carry your skeleton on the outside, to be literally enclosed by what keeps you from collapsing. The grasshopper flexes its armor and is gone; I’m left envying something that can leap five feet without once questioning the landing.

The first drop of rain evaporates before it reaches me, but I smell it again, stronger now, a mineral perfume rising from the dust the way stories sometimes rise out of the harm they were meant to silence. My grandmother believed storms were the sky reenacting old grievances: a dropped locket, a promise rephrased until it broke. She would run outside with colanders to catch the “clean part” of the rain before it touched the ground, then use it to rinse her hair, which stayed midnight-black well into her seventies. When she died, the faucets in her house kept chattering for hours, as though water itself refused to believe she wouldn’t come back with her cracked enamel bowl and her laugh that tasted of clove and thunder.

I haven’t told anyone this in years, and saying it now feels like unlocking a diary only to discover the ink has faded to whispers. But the syllables are still warm in my mouth, the way pebbles left in pocket survive body heat long after the river. I think that’s why I walk—to keep moving the way a sentence keeps moving, subject to verb to object, until a period lands like a small flat stone on the chest of something that once breathed.

To my left, corn tassels rattle warnings. Farmers say the crop’s grown taller this summer than any in memory; they speak in hushed tones, as if the fields might overhear and decide to keep rising until they blot out the bank loans. Every plant is a wager against tomorrow, rooted but gambling. I touch a stalk and feel the damp of impending rain travel up its vascular corridors, a telegram of pressure and relief. In that moment I understand that growing is not the opposite of dying but its roommate, the way two rivals share a bed in a boardinghouse, each pretending the other doesn’t snore.

The road dead-ends into a creek whose name is too small for most maps. It’s running high, milk-chocolate and fast, carrying twigs that look like sentences from a language I almost studied: the curl of an accent, the brutal simplicity of a noun. A red plastic spoon goes past, then a playing card—nine of spades—then the reflection of the clouds, which appear upside-down and bruised. I want to step into that reflection, boots filling with sky, but I stay on the bank because I promised someone once I’d try to live long enough to finish the thought, even if the thought is enormous and keeps subdividing like bread dough left overnight.

Behind me, tires hiss on the distant highway, the sound of other people’s urgency. I used to chase that sound, convinced it was the soundtrack of arrival. Now it feels more like the faint applause from an audience that’s already turned toward the exit, coats over arms, ready to drivehome through whatever dark has been scheduled. I wave anyway, a small semaphore of gratitude for strangers who keep moving so I don’t have to.

The rain decides to mean it. Drops the size of shirt buttons hit the dust, releasing tiny coronas that smell of rust and childhood. I lift my face because I’ve read that birds don’t close their eyes when they drink; they trust the weather not to drown them mid-sip. Within seconds my shirt is a second skin, inked with darker versions of itself. I realize I’m laughing, not out of triumph but because each drop feels like a syllable of lost grammar returning: you, are, here. Three words that together make a map large enough to get lost inside rather than outside.

I think of the thousand words I was given, how I thought I’d sculpt them into a monument, something that could be circled and photographed from a respectful distance. Instead I’ve let them trickle through fingers, pooling wherever the ground softens, forming this momentary muddy alphabet that will dry into strange topographies overnight. Maybe that’s enough— to witness the shape water takes when it believes it’s endless, to walk home smelling of iron and storm, pockets heavy with wet coin and the certainty that tomorrow the grass will be taller, the creek lower, the billboard still announcing the impossible obvious.

The horizon cracks open, not sunset but sheet lightning folding the day like a letter into an envelope of white. I stand inside that fold, a single sentence still being written, the ink never quite dry, the address smearing until it reads simply: Return, return.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
